ELITE HOCKEY DRILLS
8-WEEK OFF-ICE PROGRAM
Off-Ice Athletic Development for Hockey · AGES 9–11 · Fun-First Foundation

MOVE
& PLAY.

Build a fast, springy, confident young athlete — the right way.

WEEKS
3
SESSIONS / WK

@ELITE_HOCKEY_DRILLS
elitehockeydrills.com
Read This First

BUILT FORyoung players.

This is an off-ice program for hockey players roughly 9 to 11 years old. It is not a small version of an adult workout — it is built from the ground up for growing bodies, with one goal: to make training fun while quietly building the movement, balance, and speed that strong skating is built on.

At this age the best thing we can train is how a child moves. So the program is full of skipping, hopping, balancing, bounding, sprinting, and games — not heavy lifting. There is no barbell, no grinding, and nothing is ever done “to failure.” Just bands, bodyweight, a couple of light weights, a little space, and a lot of quality reps.

Every session uses the same simple shape and takes about 35–50 minutes — closer to 60 on the peak weeks. Three sessions a week, eight weeks, two four-week blocks. Block 1 teaches the body to move well and land softly. Block 2 turns that good movement into spring and speed, with plenty of competition and play.

A parent or non-specialist coach can run all of it. Every exercise has a plain-language cue and one “what good looks like” point, plus a video on the site. Your job is simple: keep it fun, keep it quality, and watch a confident athlete grow.

What’s inside

24 coach-built sessions over 8 weeks · 5 measurable skill-check tests to track real progress · a video demo for every exercise · a full glossary and easier/harder scaling for every move · programmed by an S&C coach who trains kids every week.

What to expect

Eight weeks will not — and should not — turn a child into a powerhouse. What it builds is better: softer landings, steadier balance, smoother sprinting, and a love of moving well. Those are the foundations that pay off for years on the ice. Train it as written, keep it joyful, and the skill-check pages will show the progress.

TWO RULES · NON-NEGOTIABLE
01

Every landing is soft and “stuck.” We teach the body to absorb and balance before we ask it to bounce — this is how we keep young joints safe.

02

It stays fun. Quality reps, long rest, lots of games. If it ever feels like a grind, make it lighter and more playful — the fun is what builds the athlete.

Before you begin

This program is general fitness guidance, not medical advice. Have your child cleared for physical activity by a physician before starting — especially with any injury, illness, or health condition — and accept that training carries inherent risk. Backed by a 30-day money-back guarantee.

01The Method

WHY THISworks.

This is not a random circuit. It follows long-term athletic development — the right things, in the right order, for a 9–11-year-old body.

Move well first

Before speed or strength comes skill: squatting, hinging, balancing, skipping, and landing with control. This is the golden age for learning movement, so we spend it building a clean, athletic toolkit the child will use for life.

Land like a cat

Soft, quiet, balanced landings are the single most important — and most protective — skill at this age. Every jump starts as a stick-and-freeze, so the body learns to absorb and control force long before it learns to rebound.

Coordination & speed mechanics

Young nervous systems learn fast. We groove great sprint mechanics — tall posture, knee up, quick feet — and reactive agility through games, building the athletic coordination that supports smooth, fast skating.

Spring, not grind

Power comes from low, snappy jumps and light, ballistic throws — and, in the peak weeks, a simple band-and-bodyweight ‘contrast’ pairing for extra snap. The load stays light and the focus stays on quality — never heavy grinding or training to fatigue. We build springs with quality reps and long rest, so it is safe, sharp, and never a slog.

Single-leg & groin care

Skating happens one leg at a time, so balance, single-leg control, and gentle groin work run through the whole program. Strong, mobile hips early means a more durable skater later.

Fun is the engine

Games, competition, and play are not the reward at the end — they are the method. A child who loves training will train for years, and that consistency beats any single hard session.

At this age, the goal is not a stronger kid — it is a better mover. Speed and power grow on top of that, for years.

The Method
02The Roadmap

THEeight weeks.

Two blocks of four weeks. The work builds for three weeks, then eases off — so the body soaks it up and the child stays fresh and keen.

BLOCK 1 · WEEKS 1–4

MOVE & LAND

“Move well first. Land like a cat.”

Squat, hinge, balance, skip, and soft stuck landings, plus low, quiet ankle pogos. No big bounding yet. The work builds W1→W3, then W4 is an easy week with a skills check.

BLOCK 2 · WEEKS 5–8

SPRING & PLAY

“Turn good movement into speed.”

Low bounds, broad-jump-to-sprint, light ball throws, and reactive games. Builds W5→W7 (peak), then W8 lightens up for a fun final skills check.

THE WEEKLY RHYTHMThree sessions a week, with a rest or free-play day in between. The three days rotate focus so nothing repeats and everything gets covered.
DAY 1
Mon
Run & Land
Linear speed · landing · core
DAY 2
Wed
Bound & Cut
Lateral · single-leg · power
DAY 3
Fri
Move & Play
Coordination · balance · games
WEEK 4Easy week + skills check
WEEK 8Show it + final skills check
03How To Use This

EVERYsession.

All 24 sessions share the same simple shape. Run them in order. Total time: about 35–50 minutes — closer to 60 on the peak weeks.

1
RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min

Raise, Activate, Mobilize, Potentiate — the same fun warm-up on the next page, done before every session.

2
Movement Primer6–8 min

A few low hops and soft landings to wake the body up and groove good movement. Always crisp, never tiring.

3
Speed & Skill12–16 min

The fun, fast skill of the day — done fresh. Full effort on each short rep, with long rest. Quality over quantity.

4
Strength & Control12–16 min

Bodyweight and band work, balance, and core — built one leg at a time, with control. No grinding, no failure.

5
Compete & Play5–8 min

A game or finisher to end on a high — racing, battling, skipping, or free play. This is the part they remember.

6
Cool-Down3–5 min

Slow the breath and stretch gently to start recovery before heading off.

HOW EACH EXERCISE READS

Every exercise is written the same simple way:

01 EXERCISE — NAME
The movement. The name is a live link — tap it for the full video demo and step-by-step guide on the site.
SETS × REPS / TIME
How many rounds, and reps, seconds, or metres per round. A range means do what looks good that day.
CUE · QUALITY
The one thing to focus on, and how it should feel. “X” means move fast and snappy; quiet, soft, and stuck means a controlled landing.
REST
How long to rest before the next round. Rest is long on purpose — we want fresh, fast, quality reps, not a burn.
04What You'll Need

THEkit.

Wonderfully simple. A band, a couple of light weights, a little space, and a few household bits. No gym, no heavy weights, no cost barrier — just room to move and play.

01

Loop resistance band set

Light and medium tension is plenty. The band adds gentle, safe resistance to squats, lunges, and throws — no heavy weights needed.

02

A low box or sturdy step

Shin-to-knee height. For step-ups, step-downs, and low box hop-ups with soft landings.

03

A bit of open space

A driveway, yard, hallway, or garage works for most drills — about 10 metres. The 20m sprint tests need a longer, flat stretch of roughly 30–40 m, like a park, field, or quiet street. A few cones or markers help for the games and for marking distances.

04

A skipping rope & light ball

A jump rope for footwork and fun, and a light ball for the rotational “hockey-shot” throws. A wall to throw against is handy.

05

Two light dumbbells

A light pair is plenty — or a filled water bottle or small backpack. A few moves use these; in the sessions they are shown as “DB” (dumbbell).

06

A phone timer & a helper

A phone or stopwatch to time the sprint tests, and a second person to call “go” for the reaction games. No helper around? The reaction drills work solo too — start on your own count or a phone beep.

That is it. Most of it can be done at home, in a garage, or at the park — with a band, a step, a couple of light weights, and space to play. The sprint tests just need a longer open stretch, like a park or field.

05Before Every Session

THE RAMPwarm-up.

Raise. Activate. Mobilize. Potentiate. About 8–10 minutes before every session — it wakes the body up, grooves good movement, and makes the fun stuff feel easy.

R
Raise~2 min

Get warm and breathing — heart rate up, body ready to move.

  • Jumping Jacks↗
  • Butt Kicks↗
A
Activate~2–3 min

Switch on the hips, glutes, and groin that power every stride.

  • Banded Monster Walk↗
  • Banded Lateral Walk↗
  • Banded Clamshell↗
  • Adductor Rocker↗
M
Mobilize~2–3 min

Open the hips, ankles, and upper back through full, easy range.

  • 90/90 Hip Switch↗
  • T-Spine Rotation↗
  • Spider Lunge with Rotation↗
  • Ankle Dorsiflexion (Wall)↗
  • Inchworm to Push-Up↗
P
Potentiate~2 min

A few crisp, springy reps to get fast and ready to play.

  • A-Skip in Place↗
  • Lateral Squat Shift↗
After every session · 3–5 min

THEcool-down.

Slow everything down. Quiet the breath first, then move through some gentle, easy stretches. This is when the body recovers and grows.

  • Slow Walk with Nose Breathing↗
  • Child's Pose with Breath↗
  • 90/90 Hip Stretch + T-Spine Opener↗
  • Supine Spinal Twist↗
  • Foam Roll / Tissue Work↗
  • Hip / Groin Flow↗
A quick check for parents & coaches

SAFETY &fun first.

This is a development program for young athletes — fun and good movement come before anything else. Keep these in mind every session.

  • Before starting, have your child cleared for physical activity by a physician — especially with any injury, illness, or health condition.
  • It should feel like play. If a child dreads it, lighten it and keep it fun.
  • Good shoes, a flat non-slip surface, and a clear space to move.
  • Every rep is about quality, not tiredness — never train “to failure.”
  • Stop any movement that causes sharp pain, and rest when tired or unwell.
  • An adult supervises — watch that landings stay quiet with the knees tracking over the toes, keep the box low enough to land in control, and end a set the moment form turns sloppy. The warm-up is always done before the fast or jumping work.

Kids grow and develop at different rates — that is completely normal. Scale every movement to the child in front of you (see the Make It Easier / Harder library).

Please note. This program is general fitness guidance, not medical advice, and it does not replace professional supervision or care. Training — including jumping, landing, and sprinting — carries inherent risk; by using it you accept responsibility for your child’s safe participation and agree to stop and seek advice if anything causes pain or concern. Not satisfied? Email us within 30 days for a full refund.

06The Skills Check

SEEyour progress.

Five simple, measurable tests — every one gives you a number to beat. No gym needed. Do the same five, in the same order, three times: Week 1, Week 4, and Week 8.

Always warm up with the RAMP first, use the same shoes, surface, and timing method, and keep it positive. Write down your best number — then beat it later.

01+5–10 cm

Broad Jump↗

Power · jump distance
Best of 3 · measure in cm

Stand still, swing the arms, and jump as far as you can off two feet. Stick the landing. Measure to the back heel.

8-Week Aim▲ +5–10 cm
02−0.1 to −0.3 s

20m Sprint↗

Acceleration & speed
Best of 2 · full rest · time in s

From a standing start, sprint a marked 20m as fast as you can. Time it with a phone timer or a partner.

8-Week Aim▲ −0.1 to −0.3 s
03−0.1 to −0.3 s

20m Flying Sprint↗

Top speed
Best of 2 · time in s

Take a 10–15m run-up to build speed, then time only the 20m at full speed. This tests top speed, not the start.

8-Week Aim▲ −0.1 to −0.3 s
04+15–30 s

Iso Wall Sit↗

Leg strength · max hold
1 max hold · time in s

Sit against a wall, knees at 90°, thighs flat. Hold as long as you can with good form. Stop when the hips drop.

8-Week Aim▲ +15–30 s
05+10–20 s

SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗

Balance · max hold
Best per leg · time in s

Balance on one foot with your eyes closed. Count the seconds until you wobble or touch down. Test both legs.

8-Week Aim▲ +10–20 s
3 CHECK-INS · WEEK 1 → WEEK 4 → WEEK 8

WEEK 1 — STARTING POINT

Where you begin. These are the numbers you get to beat.
Skill checkWhat it showsTry 1Try 2Try 3Best
Broad Jump↗Power · jump distance
20m Sprint↗Acceleration & speed
20m Flying Sprint↗Top speed
Iso Wall Sit↗Leg strength · max hold
SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗Balance · max hold

Write down your best number. Re-test on the same surface, shoes, and method each time.

07
BLOCK 1 · WEEKS 1–4

MOVE & LAND

“Move well first. Land like a cat.”

Block 1 is all about the basics that make a great athlete: standing strong, squatting and hinging well, balancing on one leg, skipping with good rhythm, and — most important at this age — landing softly and under control. Landings are soft and stuck, with only low, quiet pogo hops for ankle spring — the bigger bounding waits for Block 2 — so the body learns to absorb force safely first.

The work climbs gently for three weeks, then Week 4 is an easy week with a quick skills check. Nothing is to failure. Quality, fun, and confidence are the whole point.

BLOCK 1

WEEK 1

BUILD

Learn the moves. Soft landings, tall running, and fun. Keep every rep crisp — quality beats tired.

BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 1 · LEARN IT
DAY 1Mon
Run Tall, Land SoftLinear speed · landing · core
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer4–7 min
01 Ankle Hop Stick↗
2×6 X · stiff ankle · quiet REST 45s
Hop from the ankles only. Land quiet and freeze.
02 Drop Squat↗
3×3 X · catch & stick REST 45s
Drop into a strong stance fast, then freeze balanced.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 Wall Drive Hold↗
3×10s/side tall · drive knee REST 45s
Lean on a wall, tall body, drive one knee up and hold.
02 A-Skip Moving↗
3×10m X · knee up, toe up REST 45s
Skip forward — knee up, toe up, punch the arms.
4 Strength & Control11–13 min
01 Banded Squat↗
3×8 smooth · knees out REST 60s
Knees push out, tall chest, stand up strong.
02 Band Glute Bridge↗
2×10 squeeze the top REST 45s
Push the hips up, squeeze, hold a beat at the top.
03 Pallof March↗
2×6/side don’t twist REST 45s
March tall — don’t let the band twist you.
04 Side Plank with Reach↗
2×15s/side hips high REST 45s
Hips high, reach under and through, stay long.
5 Compete & Play3–6 min
01 Mirror Game / Free Play↗
3–4 min play speed · react
Mirror a partner or play tag. Quick feet, have fun.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 1 · LEARN IT
DAY 2Wed
Push & Stick SidewaysLateral · landing · single-leg
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer4–7 min
01 Low Box Hop-Up — Stick↗
3×4 X · land soft · stick REST 45s
Hop up onto the box, land soft, stick it, step down.
02 Skater Hop — Stick It↗
2×3/side X · stick 2s REST 45s
Push sideways, land on one leg, freeze like a statue.
3 Speed & Skill5–8 min
01 Crossover Step↗
3×20–30s cross over · both ways REST 60s
Crossover one way, then plant and come back the other — low and quick both ways.
02 Reactive 5m Sprint↗
4×5m X · react then go REST 45s
Wait for the signal, then explode the first three steps.
4 Strength & Control14–17 min
01 Box Step-Up (DBs)↗
3×6/side drive through foot REST 60s
Stand tall off the top foot. Lower with control.
02 Banded Lateral Lunge↗
3×6/side sit into the hip REST 60s
Sit back into one hip, push the floor away to return.
03 Banded Standing Adduction↗
2×8/side squeeze inner thigh REST 45s
Pull the leg in across the body. Stay tall.
04 Dead Bug with DB↗
2×6/side ribs down REST 45s
Lower the opposite arm and leg slowly. Low back stays down.
5 Compete & Play3–5 min
01 Jump Rope — Steady↗
3×30s quiet feet REST 30s
Light, quiet bounces on the balls of the feet.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 1 · LEARN IT
DAY 3Fri
Move & PlayCoordination · balance · games
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer3–5 min
01 Bear Crawl Variations↗
2×8m hips low · steady REST 45s
Crawl with opposite hand and foot, hips low and level.
02 Drop Squat↗
2×3 X · stick REST 45s
Snap into a strong landing and freeze.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 Fast Feet / Ladder Equiv↗
3×6s quick feet REST 45s
Fast, light feet on the spot or over lines. Stay tall.
02 Reactive Lateral Start↗
4×5m X · react · first step REST 45s
React to the call, push off hard, sprint 5m.
4 Strength & Control10–12 min
01 Deep Squat Hold↗
3×20s sit tall REST 45s
Sit into a deep squat, chest tall, heels down.
02 SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
2×20s/side still & balanced REST 30s
Balance on one foot, eyes closed, stay quiet.
03 Plank Shoulder Tap↗
2×8/side no hip wobble REST 45s
Tap the opposite shoulder. Keep the hips still.
04 Hollow Rock↗
2×8 low back down REST 45s
Press the low back into the floor and rock gently.
5 Compete & Play3–6 min
01 Free Play / 1v1 Game↗
4 min play speed · compete
Small-space 1v1 or free play. Compete and smile.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1

WEEK 2

BUILD

Build on Week 1 with a little more. Same key skills, new twists — own the positions before you speed them up.

BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 2 · BUILD
DAY 1Mon
Run Tall, Land SoftLinear speed · landing · core
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer5–8 min
01 Ankle Hop Stick↗
2×8 X · stiff ankle · quiet REST 45s
Quiet ankle hops. Freeze the last one.
02 Depth Drop (Low Box)↗
3×3 X · land soft · stick REST 60s
Step off the low box, land soft and stick. No bounce.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 Wall Drive Hold↗
3×12s/side tall · drive knee REST 45s
Tall body, drive the knee, hold steady.
02 A-Skip + March Combo↗
3×12m X · rhythm REST 45s
March then skip in rhythm — toe up, tall posture.
4 Strength & Control12–15 min
01 Banded Front Squat↗
3×8 smooth · tall chest REST 60s
Band at the shoulders, sit tall, stand up strong.
02 SL RDL (Banded)↗
2×6/side flat back · balance REST 60s
Hinge on one leg, flat back, feel the hamstring.
03 Banded Chop / Lift↗
2×8/side move from the trunk REST 45s
Turn from the belly, arms stay long. Control it back.
04 Side Plank with Reach↗
2×18s/side hips high REST 45s
Long line, hips high, reach through.
5 Compete & Play3–6 min
01 Mirror Game / Free Play↗
4 min react · fun
Mirror / tag game. React and change direction.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 2 · BUILD
DAY 2Wed
Push & Stick SidewaysLateral · landing · single-leg
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer6–9 min
01 Lateral Skater Bound↗
3×3/side X · stick REST 60s
Bound sideways, land on one leg, stick and hold.
02 Single-Leg Pogo Hops↗
2×6/side X · quick · quiet REST 45s
Small quick hops on one foot, stay tall and quiet.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 Crossover Step to Sprint↗
3×8m cross · then sprint REST 60s
Crossover step, then burst into a short sprint.
02 Reactive Mirror Sprint↗
3×5m X · mirror · react REST 45s
Mirror your partner’s move, then race 5m.
4 Strength & Control14–16 min
01 SL Box Step-Down↗
3×6/side slow · control REST 60s
Lower slowly off the box, tap, then stand back up.
02 Banded Lateral Lunge↗
3×6/side sit into the hip REST 60s
Sit into the hip, drive back against the band.
03 Banded RDL↗
2×8 flat back · hinge REST 45s
Push the hips back, flat back, stand tall against the band.
04 Tall Plank DB Drag↗
2×6/side no hip wobble REST 45s
Drag a light weight across. Keep the hips dead still.
5 Compete & Play3–5 min
01 Jump Rope — Intervals↗
4×20s quick · light REST 30s
Quick light bounces, rest, repeat.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 2 · BUILD
DAY 3Fri
Move & PlayCoordination · balance · games
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer4–7 min
01 Multi-Direction Bound↗
2×4 X · stick each REST 60s
Bound forward, side, and back — stick every landing.
02 Ankle Hop Stick↗
2×8 X · quiet REST 45s
Stiff, quiet ankle hops.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 A-Skip + B-Skip↗
3×10m X · knee up · paw back REST 45s
A-skip then B-skip: knee up, then reach and paw back.
02 Reactive 4-Cone↗
3×20–30s X · react · low cuts REST 60s
React to the call, sprint to the cone, stay low in cuts.
4 Strength & Control10–13 min
01 Iso Wall Sit↗
3×25s tall · still REST 45s
Sit against the wall, knees at 90°, hold strong.
02 SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
2×25s/side still REST 30s
One-foot balance, eyes closed.
03 Push-Up to Reach↗
2×5/side hips square REST 45s
Push up, then reach one arm long without twisting.
04 Hollow Rock↗
2×10 low back down REST 45s
Hollow rock — low back pinned to the floor.
5 Compete & Play3–6 min
01 Battle Game / 1v1↗
4 min compete · controlled
Friendly 1v1 battle for a ball or space. Controlled and fun.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1

WEEK 3

PEAK

The biggest week of Block 1. A touch more work — but every rep still fast, soft, and under control.

BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 3 · PEAK
DAY 1Mon
Run Tall, Land SoftLinear speed · landing · core
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer6–8 min
01 High-Low Pogo Combo↗
3×8 X · quick · quiet REST 45s
Mix small and slightly bigger pogo hops, stay springy.
02 Drop Squat↗
3×3 X · stick REST 45s
Drop and freeze in a strong stance.
3 Speed & Skill6–8 min
01 A-Skip Moving↗
4×12m X · knee up · tall REST 45s
Skip with intent — knee up, toe up, tall and quick.
02 Resisted → Free Sprint↗
3×8m X · drive · then free REST 60s
A few light-resisted steps, then sprint free.
4 Strength & Control17–19 min
01 Banded Squat↗
3×8 smooth · drive up REST 60s
Band squat — knees out, drive up tall.
02 DB Goblet Lateral Lunge↗
3×6/side sit into the hip REST 60s
Hold a light weight, sit into one hip, push back up.
03 Pallof March↗
3×8/side don’t twist REST 45s
March tall, resist the band’s twist.
04 Side Plank with Reach↗
3×20s/side hips high REST 45s
Side plank reach — long and strong.
5 Compete & Play5–8 min
01 Sprint Intervals 15s↗
4×15s near full · long rest REST 75s
Short fast runs, then full rest. Quality beats tired.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →

3×3/side X · stick 2s REST 60s
Skater hop — land and freeze on one leg.
02 Low Box Hop-Up — Stick↗
3×4 X · stick REST 45s
Hop up, land soft, stick, step down.
3 Speed & Skill7–9 min
01 Crossover Step↗
3×20–30s cross over · both ways REST 60s
Crossover one way, then plant and come back the other — low and quick both ways.
02 Reactive Lateral Start↗
4×5m X · react · burst REST 45s
React, push off, burst 5m.
4 Strength & Control19–22 min
01 Box Step-Up (DBs)↗
3×8/side drive through foot REST 60s
Step-up — stand tall, lower slowly.
02 Banded Lateral Lunge↗
3×8/side sit into the hip REST 60s
Lateral lunge — drive back against the band.
03 Banded Standing Adduction↗
3×8/side squeeze inner thigh REST 45s
Standing adduction — tall and controlled.
04 Banded Chop / Lift↗
3×8/side from the trunk REST 45s
Chop / lift from the trunk, hips still.

01 Jump Rope — Speed↗
4×20s fast feet REST 40s
Fast skipping for a short burst, then rest.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 3 · PEAK
DAY 3Fri
Move & PlayCoordination · balance · games
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer7–9 min
01 Crossover Step Bound↗
3×3/side X · stick REST 60s
Crossover bound sideways — stick the landing.
02 Single-Leg Pogo Hops↗
2×8/side X · quiet REST 45s
Single-leg pogo — quick and quiet.
3 Speed & Skill7–9 min
01 Hill Stride↗
4×15m tall · drive REST 60s
Stride up a gentle hill — tall posture, drive the arms.
02 Reactive 4-Cone↗
3×20–30s X · react · low REST 60s
Reactive 4-cone — stay low and sharp.
4 Strength & Control13–16 min
01 SL Box Step-Down↗
3×6/side slow · control REST 60s
Step-down with control.
02 SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
3×25s/side still REST 30s
Eyes-closed balance — longer hold.
03 Plank to Push-Up↗
2×6 tight body REST 45s
Plank up to hands and back — body stays in a line.
04 Plank Shoulder Tap↗
2×10/side no wobble REST 45s
Shoulder taps — hips locked.
5 Compete & Play4–7 min
01 Free Play with Intent↗
5 min compete · play hard
Free play with a goal — compete hard, stay creative.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1

WEEK 4

EASY

Easy week. Less work on purpose — we cut the number of sets, not the snap, so the body soaks it up. Keep the few reps sharp, then do a quick skills check.

BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 4 · EASY WEEK
DAY 1Mon
Run Tall, Land SoftLighter · keep it crisp
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer3–6 min
01 Ankle Hop Stick↗
2×6 X · quiet REST 45s
Easy, quiet ankle hops.
02 Drop Squat↗
2×3 X · stick REST 45s
Drop and freeze. Crisp, not tired.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 A-Skip Moving↗
3×10m X · smooth REST 45s
Smooth, tall A-skips. Quality only.
02 20m Flying Sprint↗
3×1 X · build · fast 20m REST 60s
Build up, then run a fast, relaxed 20m.
4 Strength & Control7–10 min
01 Banded Squat↗
2×8 smooth REST 60s
Band squat — easy and crisp.
02 Band Glute Bridge↗
2×10 squeeze REST 45s
Glute bridge — squeeze and hold.
03 Pallof March↗
2×6/side don’t twist REST 45s
Pallof march — easy.
5 Compete & Play3–6 min
01 Mirror Game / Free Play↗
4 min react · fun
Mirror / tag — just for fun this week.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 4 · EASY WEEK
DAY 2Wed
Push & Stick SidewaysLighter · keep it crisp
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer3–6 min
01 Skater Hop — Stick It↗
2×3/side X · stick REST 45s
Skater hop — stick it. Quality.
02 Low Box Hop-Up — Stick↗
2×4 X · stick REST 45s
Box hop-up — soft and stuck.
3 Speed & Skill5–7 min
01 Crossover Step↗
3×20–30s cross over · both ways REST 60s
Crossover one way, then plant and come back the other — low and quick both ways.
02 Reactive Mirror Sprint↗
3×5m X · react REST 45s
Mirror and race — light and fun.
4 Strength & Control8–10 min
01 Box Step-Up (DBs)↗
2×6/side tall REST 60s
Step-up — controlled.
02 Banded Lateral Lunge↗
2×6/side sit into the hip REST 60s
Lateral lunge — easy.
03 Side Plank with Reach↗
2×15s/side hips high REST 45s
Side plank reach — short hold.
5 Compete & Play3–5 min
01 Jump Rope — Steady↗
3×30s quiet feet REST 30s
Easy, steady skipping.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →
BLOCK 1 · MOVE & LAND · WEEK 4 · EASY WEEK
DAY 3Fri
Skills Check + PlayLight testing · games
1 · RAMP Warm-Up8–10 min
Raise · Activate · Mobilize · Potentiate — the same fun warm-up before every session. see page →
2 Movement Primer3–5 min
01 Bear Crawl Variations↗
2×8m hips low REST 45s
Bear crawl — smooth.
02 Ankle Hop Stick↗
2×6 X · quiet REST 45s
Quiet ankle hops.
3 Speed & Skill9–12 min
01 Broad Jump↗
best of 3 jump far · stick it REST full
Broad-jump test — stand still, jump as far as you can off two feet, stick it. Measure to the back heel; record your best.
02 20m Sprint↗
best of 2 standing start · full effort REST full
Sprint test — from a standing start, sprint a marked 20m. Time it; record your best.
03 20m Flying Sprint↗
best of 2 10–15m run-in · top speed REST full
Top-speed test — take a 10–15m run-up, then time only the 20m at full speed; record your best.
4 Strength & Control5–8 min
01 Iso Wall Sit↗
1 max hold knees 90° · hold REST full
Wall-sit test — knees at 90°, sit tall, hold as long as you can. Count the seconds.
02 SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
best per leg eyes closed · count REST 30s
Balance test — one foot, eyes closed. Count seconds until you wobble; test both legs.
5 Compete & Play4–7 min
01 Free Play / 1v1 Game↗
5 min compete · fun
Free play / 1v1 — enjoy the easy week.
6 · Cool-Down3–5 min
Slow the breath and stretch gently to finish — that is when the body grows. see page →

WEEK 4 — CHECK-IN

Halfway. Re-do the five tests and see what moved.
Skill checkWhat it showsTry 1Try 2Try 3Best
Broad Jump↗Power · jump distance
20m Sprint↗Acceleration & speed
20m Flying Sprint↗Top speed
Iso Wall Sit↗Leg strength · max hold
SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗Balance · max hold

Compare to Week 1. Even a small gain — farther, faster, or longer — counts.

08
BLOCK 2 · WEEKS 5–8

SPRING & PLAY

“Now turn good movement into speed.”

With the basics owned, Block 2 adds spring: lateral bounds, broad jumps into sprints, light ball throws for a hockey-shot feel, and reactive change-of-direction games. Landings are still soft, volume is still low, and rest on the jumps and sprints is still long — we are building springs, not grinding. The one exception is a short, shift-style conditioning finisher in the peak weeks, where the goal is to stay fast even as you tire — like a real hockey shift, kept brief and game-like.

The work peaks in Week 7 with everything at game speed, then Week 8 lightens up so you feel fast and fresh for a final, fun skills check. Eight weeks, one confident young athlete.

BLOCK 2

WEEK 5

BUILD

New block: now we add spring. Bounds, light throws, and a first taste of contrast power — all soft and snappy.

Skills CheckDo the 5 measured tests

01 Ankle Hop Stick↗
2×6 X · quiet REST 45s
Warm, springy hops before you test.
02 Drop Squat↗
2×3 X · stick REST 45s
A few soft, stuck landings to wake the legs up.
3 Speed & Skill9–12 min
01 Broad Jump↗
best of 3 jump far · stick it REST full
Broad-jump test — stand still, jump as far as you can off two feet, stick it. Measure to the back heel; record your best.
02 20m Sprint↗
best of 2 standing start · full effort REST full
Sprint test — from a standing start, sprint a marked 20m. Time it; record your best.
03 20m Flying Sprint↗
best of 2 10–15m run-in · top speed REST full
Top-speed test — take a 10–15m run-up, then time only the 20m at full speed; record your best.

01 Iso Wall Sit↗
2×max hold strong REST 60s
Wall-sit test — knees at 90°, sit tall, hold as long as you can. Count seconds.
02 SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
2×max/side count seconds REST 30s
Balance test — one foot, eyes closed. Count seconds, both legs.

01 Combine Circuit↗
1–2 rounds fun · move well
Combine circuit challenge — celebrate the work, have fun.

WEEK 8 — YOU vs YOU

The proof. The same five tests, eight weeks apart.
Skill checkWeek 1Week 8ChangeBetter?Aim
Broad Jump↗+5–10 cm
20m Sprint↗−0.1 to −0.3 s
20m Flying Sprint↗−0.1 to −0.3 s
Iso Wall Sit↗+15–30 s
SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗+10–20 s

Farther, faster, or longer — every one is a win. Tag your progress and celebrate it.

Move well, land soft, then go fast. That is how you build an athlete who lasts.

Coach David Ciboch
13Exercise Glossary

EVERYmove.

Every exercise in the program, with one quick cue. Each name links to its full video demo and step-by-step guide on the site.

A few short names you’ll see: SL = single-leg · CMJ = countermovement (two-foot) jump · RDL = Romanian deadlift (a hip hinge) · Iso = isometric (a still hold) · DB = dumbbell.

Jumps, Speed & Agility

A-Skip + B-Skip↗
Travel forward alternating A-skips (knee driven to hip height, toe up) with B-skips (from the high knee, actively extend the lower leg out, then “paw” it back down and under the hip).
Speed & Agility
A-Skip + March Combo↗
Alternate between A-march (a slow, deliberate marching version emphasizing positions — high knee, toe up, tall posture) and A-skip (the rhythmic skipping version) in a prescribed sequence as you travel forward.
Speed & Agility
A-Skip Moving↗
Skip forward driving one knee to hip height with a dorsiflexed ankle (toe up), the opposite arm driving in a 1-2 rhythm, striking the ground actively under the hip on the balls of the feet.
Speed & Agility
A-Skip Resisted↗
Wear a belt/band anchored behind (or held by a partner) and perform A-skips moving forward against the resistance — driving the knee to hip height with a dorsiflexed ankle, the opposite arm punching, against the band’s backward pull.
Speed & Agility
Ankle Hop Stick↗
From a tall stance, perform a stiff-ankle hop (mostly from the ankles, minimal knee bend), then land on the balls of the feet and stick the landing quietly under control, holding balanced.
Plyometric
Band Rotational Scoop Toss↗
Anchor a band low to the side. In an athletic stance, hold it low and to the trail side, wind into the back hip, then explosively rotate and “scoop” the band up and across the body diagonally (low-to-high), driving from the hips and legs through the trunk.
Rotational
Banded Rotational Press↗
Anchor a band to the side at chest height. In an athletic stance side-on, hold the band at the chest and press it forward while rotating the trunk and hips away from the anchor, finishing with the arms extended and the body turned through.
Rotational
Banded Rotational Throw (Wall)↗
Anchor a band to the side. Hold it at the trunk in an athletic side-on stance, wind into the back hip against the band, then explosively rotate and release toward the wall — or drive into a hard rotational follow-through — driving the hips and trunk.
Rotational
Banded Squat Jump↗
Stand on a band (or with it looped over the shoulders), feet shoulder-width. Dip into a quarter squat, then jump explosively against the band’s resistance, fighting it all the way to full extension.
Plyometric
Broad Jump → Sprint 10m↗
Perform a maximal broad jump, and on landing immediately transition into a 10-metre acceleration sprint without a pause — converting the landing into the first driving strides.
Plyometric
Continuous Lateral Bound↗
Bound laterally from one leg to the other in a continuous chain, pushing off explosively and landing on the outside leg with a soft, athletic knee, then immediately driving back the other way.
Plyometric
Contrast Jump↗
Perform a heavier or resisted effort (e.g., a loaded jump or banded squat jump) for a few reps, then after a short rest immediately perform a few maximal unloaded jumps (e.
Plyometric
Crossover Step↗
From an athletic stance, crossover-step a few metres to one side — hips open, trailing leg crossing over with low, quick steps — then plant and crossover back the other way, shuffling side to side for the set.
Speed & Agility
Crossover Step Bound↗
Using a crossover pattern, push off and bound laterally while the trailing leg crosses over in front, then plant and push off again, continuing across the floor.
Plyometric
Crossover Step to Sprint↗
From an athletic stance, open the hips and crossover step laterally, then transition the crossover into a forward acceleration, sprinting out for several metres.
Speed & Agility
Depth Drop (Low Box)↗
Stand on a low box, step (don’t jump) off the edge, and land on both feet in an athletic position — knees bent, hips back, weight on the whole foot.
Plyometric
Drop Squat↗
Stand tall, then quickly drop the body down into an athletic quarter-squat landing position — feet snapping out to landing width, hips back, knees bent and tracking out — absorbing the descent and freezing in a stable, braced stance.
Plyometric
Fast Feet / Ladder Equiv↗
Using an agility ladder (or imagined rungs/lines), perform quick-feet patterns — one foot in each box, two feet in, lateral in-outs — moving the feet as fast as possible with short, light ground contacts on the balls of the feet.
Speed & Agility
20m Flying Sprint↗
Take a 10–15-metre run-in to build speed, then time a marked 20-metre zone at maximal velocity with tall, relaxed, powerful mechanics before decelerating.
Speed & Agility
High-Low Pogo Combo↗
Alternate between low, fast pogo hops (small amplitude, very quick contacts) and higher pogo hops (greater amplitude) in a prescribed rhythm — for example, several quick low hops then a few high hops.
Plyometric
Hill Stride↗
Find a moderate incline. Stride up it at a controlled, submaximal effort with tall posture, strong knee drive, and active arm action, emphasizing clean mechanics and powerful ground contact rather than all-out speed.
Speed & Agility
Jump Rope — Intervals↗
Alternate harder, faster skipping bursts with easier recovery skipping (or rest) on a prescribed work-to-rest ratio — fast skipping for a set time, then easy skipping or rest, repeated.
Conditioning
Jump Rope — Speed↗
Perform short bursts of maximal-speed skipping — turning the rope and moving the feet as fast as possible (fast single-unders or double-unders) for a brief duration, then resting.
Conditioning
Jump Rope — Steady↗
Skip rope at a steady, sustainable pace, staying tall on the balls of the feet with small, quick hops and the wrists turning the rope (not the arms).
Conditioning
Lateral Skater Bound↗
Bound laterally from one leg to the other in a continuous, rhythmic pattern, swinging the arms and pushing off explosively each time.
Plyometric
Low Box Hop-Up — Stick↗
Stand facing a low box, dip and explosively jump up onto the box, landing softly on both feet in an athletic position and sticking the landing quietly on top.
Plyometric
Mirror Sprint Game↗
Pair up facing a partner across a line or in a small box. One leads with unpredictable movements — sprinting, shuffling, changing direction — while the follower mirrors as fast and accurately as possible; score points for the leader escaping or the follower staying glued.
Speed & Agility
Multi-Direction Bound↗
Bound explosively in varied directions on a prescribed pattern — forward, laterally, and diagonally — landing softly on one or both legs and controlling each landing before the next bound.
Plyometric
Reactive 4-Cone↗
Set four cones in a square or diamond around a central start. From an athletic stance at the center, react to a cue indicating a cone and sprint/cut to it, then return to center and react to the next cue.
Speed & Agility
Reactive 5m Sprint↗
From an athletic ready stance, react to a cue (a coach’s call, a hand signal, or a partner’s movement) and sprint maximally for 5 metres, driving the arms and leaning into the acceleration.
Speed & Agility
Reactive Lateral Start↗
From an athletic ready stance, react to a cue (call, signal, or partner) and explode laterally in the indicated direction using a crossover or drop step, accelerating hard for a few metres.
Speed & Agility
Reactive Mirror Sprint↗
Face a partner a few metres apart. The partner moves unpredictably (forward, lateral, or backward), and you mirror their movement as fast and accurately as possible, sprinting and changing direction to match.
Speed & Agility
Repeat Efforts — Shift Simulation↗
This recreates the work-then-rest rhythm of a real hockey shift, and you can do it entirely on your own.
Conditioning
Repeat Sprint Ability↗
Perform repeated short maximal sprints (5–10 seconds each) with short recovery between, repeating for the prescribed number.
Conditioning
Resisted → Free Sprint↗
Sprint several metres against a band or sled resistance, then have the resistance released (or run out of a held band) and immediately continue into a free, unresisted sprint — feeling light and fast as the resistance disappears.
Speed & Agility
Rotational Ball Side Toss↗
Stand side-on to a wall, athletic stance, holding the light ball low and to the trail side.
Rotational
Rotational Ball Toss↗
Stand side-on to a wall in an athletic stance, holding a light ball at the trunk.
Rotational
Single-Leg Pogo Hops↗
Standing on one leg, hop continuously straight up using mostly the ankle, with the knee nearly straight and stiff.
Plyometric
Skater Hop — Stick It↗
From a single-leg athletic stance, push off explosively sideways and bound onto the opposite leg, swinging the arms for momentum.
Plyometric
SL CMJ Stick↗
Standing on one leg, perform a quick countermovement dip and explosively jump straight up on that single leg, swinging the arms.
Plyometric
Sprint Intervals 15s↗
Sprint maximally (running, bike, or skating-pattern work) for 15 seconds, then rest for the prescribed recovery, repeating for the set number of intervals.
Conditioning
Squat to Broad Jump↗
Descend into a full (or near-parallel) squat, hold the bottom briefly to remove the stretch reflex, then explosively extend and throw the arms forward to broad jump for distance.
Plyometric
Squat to Tuck Jump↗
From a squat, explosively jump straight up and quickly tuck both knees toward the chest at the peak, then extend the legs to land softly on the whole foot with bent knees.
Plyometric
Wall Drive Hold↗
Stand an arm’s length from a wall, lean forward with the body in a straight line from head to heel at roughly 45°, hands on the wall, and drive one knee up so the thigh is parallel and the ankle dorsiflexed (toe up), the support leg fully extended.
Speed & Agility

Strength, Squat & Single-Leg

Band Glute Bridge↗
On your back, knees bent, feet flat, mini-band above the knees. Press the knees outward against the band.
Hinge
Banded Front Squat↗
Stand on a long band, feet shoulder-width, and rack the band across the front of the shoulders (elbows high).
Squat
Banded Lateral Lunge↗
Loop a band for resistance (around the working leg or held) and stand tall. Step wide to one side, bending that knee and pushing the hips back while the trailing leg stays straight, feet flat.
Single-Leg
Banded RDL↗
Stand on a band, feet hip-width, holding the other end at the hips or shoulders.
Hinge
Banded Squat↗
Stand on a band, feet shoulder-width, holding the other end at shoulder height (or looped over shoulders).
Squat
Banded Standing Adduction↗
Anchor a band at ankle height to a post and loop it around the inside ankle of the working leg; stand side-on so the band pulls the leg outward.
Single-Leg
Box Step-Up (DBs)↗
Hold DBs at the sides, standing facing a knee-height box. Place one whole foot on the box, drive through that heel to stand tall on top (bringing the trailing leg up or into a knee drive).
Single-Leg
DB Goblet Lateral Lunge↗
Hold a DB at the chest (goblet). Step wide to one side, bending that knee and pushing the hips back while the trailing leg stays straight, both feet flat and toes forward.
Single-Leg
Deep Squat Hold↗
Lower into the bottom of a deep squat — hips below parallel — feet flat, heels down, knees pushed out.
Squat
Iso Wall Sit↗
Back flat against a wall, slide down until hips and knees are at ~90°, thighs parallel.
Squat
SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
Stand on one leg with the other foot lifted, find a tall posture, then close the eyes.
Single-Leg
SL Box Step-Down↗
Stand on top of a low box on one leg, the other leg hanging off the edge. Slowly bend the standing leg to lower the free heel toward the floor under control, keeping the standing knee tracking over the foot and the hips level.
Single-Leg
SL RDL (Banded)↗
Anchor a band underfoot of the standing leg (or to the working hip) and stand on one leg.
Hinge

Upper Body

Plank to Push-Up↗
Start in a forearm plank, body straight and braced. Press up onto one hand, then the other, into a full push-up position, then lower back down to the forearms one arm at a time.
Push
Push-Up to Reach↗
Perform a push-up; at the top, lift one hand and reach it forward (or out in front) while bracing hard to keep the hips square, then return the hand and repeat on the other side.
Push

Core & Balance

Banded Chop / Lift↗
Anchor a band high for a chop (or low for a lift). In a tall- or half-kneeling stance, grip the band with both hands and pull it diagonally across the body — high-to-low for a chop, low-to-high for a lift — keeping the arms relatively straight and moving from the trunk.
Core
Bear Crawl Variations↗
From the bear position (knees hovering an inch off the floor, back flat, hips at shoulder height), perform crawling variations — moving laterally, backward, or adding a shoulder tap or reach between steps.
Core
Dead Bug with DB↗
Lie on your back, knees bent 90° over the hips, holding a DB (or DBs) pressed toward the ceiling.
Core
Hollow Rock↗
Get into a hollow dish position (low back pressed, shoulders and legs lifted, arms overhead).
Core
Pallof March↗
Anchor a band at chest height to the side; hold it pressed out (or at the chest) and stand tall, hips and shoulders square.
Core
Plank Shoulder Tap↗
Start in a high plank, feet about hip-width (wider is easier), body in a straight line and braced.
Core
Side Plank with Reach↗
From a side plank with the hips high, reach the top arm up, then thread it under the torso and through, rotating the upper back, before opening it back up to the ceiling.
Core
Tall Plank DB Drag↗
In a high plank with the feet wide, set a DB just outside one hand. Brace hard, then reach the opposite hand across and drag the DB under the body to the other side, keeping the hips as square and level as possible.
Core

Warm-Up, Mobility & Cool-Down

90/90 Hip Stretch + T-Spine Opener↗
Sit in the 90/90 position (one leg bent in front at 90°, the other out to the side at 90°).
Recovery
90/90 Hip Switch↗
Sit with one leg bent in front (shin across, 90°) and the other bent to the side (90°), both knees down.
Warm-Up
A-Skip in Place↗
Standing tall, skip in place driving one knee to hip height while the opposite arm drives forward in a 1-2 rhythm.
Warm-Up
Adductor Rocker↗
On hands and knees, extend one leg straight out to the side, foot flat and toes forward, hips level.
Warm-Up
Ankle Dorsiflexion (Wall)↗
Half-kneeling or staggered stance facing a wall, front foot a few inches away. Keeping the heel flat, drive the front knee forward over the toes toward the wall without the heel lifting.
Warm-Up
Banded Clamshell↗
Side-lying, hips and knees bent ~45°, mini-band above the knees, feet stacked. Keeping feet together and pelvis stable (don’t roll back), lift the top knee against the band by rotating from the hip.
Warm-Up
Banded Lateral Walk↗
Mini-band above knees or ankles. Drop into a quarter-squat athletic stance with band tension. Step one foot out to the side, then bring the trailing foot part-way in — keep constant tension.
Warm-Up
Banded Monster Walk↗
Mini-band around ankles or above the knees. In an athletic quarter-squat, step diagonally forward-and-out with one foot, then the other, “walking” forward against constant tension.
Warm-Up
Butt Kicks↗
Jog in place or move forward, flicking the heels up toward the glutes with a quick hamstring contraction.
Warm-Up
Child's Pose with Breath↗
Kneel and sit the hips back toward the heels, reaching the arms forward and lowering the chest toward the floor, forehead resting down.
Recovery
Foam Roll / Tissue Work↗
Use a foam roller (or ball) to slowly roll over the major muscle groups — quads, hamstrings, glutes, calves, and back — pausing on tender spots and breathing slowly to let the tissue relax.
Recovery
Hip / Groin Flow↗
Move through a flowing sequence of hip and groin mobility movements — deep squat holds, adductor rockers, hip circles, and gentle lunges with rotation — transitioning smoothly between positions and breathing throughout.
Recovery
Inchworm to Push-Up↗
Stand tall, hinge at the hips and place hands on the floor (bend knees as needed).
Warm-Up
Jumping Jacks↗
Stand tall, feet together, arms at sides. Jump the feet out wide while sweeping the arms overhead, then jump back as the arms return.
Warm-Up
Lateral Squat Shift↗
Stand wide, toes mostly forward. Shift weight to one side, bending that knee and pushing the hips back while the opposite leg stays straight.
Warm-Up
Slow Walk with Nose Breathing↗
Walk slowly and easily for the prescribed duration, breathing only through the nose — slow, deep, controlled inhales and exhales.
Recovery
Spider Lunge with Rotation↗
From a plank, step one foot to the outside of the same-side hand into a deep lunge, back leg long and hips low.
Warm-Up
Supine Spinal Twist↗
Lie on your back, draw one knee up and across the body toward the floor on the opposite side, extending the same-side arm out and turning the head the other way.
Recovery
T-Spine Rotation↗
In quadruped or half-kneeling, place one hand behind the head. Rotate that elbow down and under toward the opposite arm, then open it up and back toward the ceiling, following the elbow with your eyes.
Warm-Up

Compete & Play

Battle Game / 1v1↗
Two players engage in a 1v1 battle for possession or position — protecting an object/ball while the other tries to win it, or competing for a spot using body position and leverage.
Compete
Combine Circuit↗
Set up 4–6 simple stations and move through them in order, on your own. For a joint-friendly version, use low-impact stations — for example a loaded carry (suitcase or farmer carry), an anti-rotation core hold (Pallof press or side plank), a single-leg balance hold, a few soft-landing hops (ankle hops or stuck skater hops), and a steady bodyweight movement (squats or step-ups).
Compete
Free Play / 1v1 Game↗
Two players compete one-on-one in a small-space game — trying to reach a target, win possession of a ball/object, or beat the other to a spot — using movement, reaction, and effort to win.
Compete
Free Play with Intent↗
Engage in free, game-like play but with a specific intent or constraint emphasized by the coach — focusing on quick first steps, staying low, reacting fast, or using a particular movement.
Compete
Mirror Game / Free Play↗
In a small space, players engage in a mirror or free-play game — mirroring a partner’s movements, playing tag, or freely moving and reacting in a competitive but unstructured way.
Compete
14Make It Easier / Harder

SCALE ITright.

Every child is different. For each main move: a simpler version if it is not clean yet, and a harder one once it is. Earn the next step — there is no rush.

Movement
Easier
Harder
Banded Squat↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a plain bodyweight squat.
HARDER Heavier band, or a slow lower into a fast stand-up.
SL RDL (Banded)↗
EASIER Touch the back toe down for balance, or use a lighter band.
HARDER Lighter touch, longer reach, or a short hold at the bottom.
Box Step-Up (DBs)↗
EASIER Lower box, or hold a support.
HARDER Higher box (within control), or a slow lower.
Banded Lateral Lunge↗
EASIER Smaller range, or bodyweight only.
HARDER Heavier band, or a slow lower into an explosive drive out.
Drop Squat↗
EASIER Drop to a smaller, slower squat.
HARDER Drop faster and lower into a deeper, snappier stick.
Skater Hop — Stick It↗
EASIER Smaller hop, or step to reset between reps.
HARDER Bound farther, or hold the stick longer.
Low Box Hop-Up — Stick↗
EASIER Lower box, or a low step.
HARDER Slightly higher box, or stick on one leg.
Broad Jump → Sprint 10m↗
EASIER Shorter jump, or step back to reset.
HARDER Jump farther, or sprint a touch longer after.
Push-Up to Reach↗
EASIER From the knees, or hands on a box.
HARDER Slower reach, or feet a little wider for balance.
Pallof March↗
EASIER Stand farther from the anchor, or lighter band.
HARDER Step closer, heavier band, or march slower.
Side Plank with Reach↗
EASIER From the knees, or a shorter hold.
HARDER Longer hold, or reach a little farther under.
Ankle Hop Stick↗
EASIER Smaller hop, or land softly on two feet.
HARDER A little higher, or hop and land on one foot.
Wall Drive Hold↗
EASIER Shorter holds, or stand a bit more upright.
HARDER Switch the driving leg, or trade legs back and forth.
A-Skip Moving↗
EASIER March it out, or skip on the spot.
HARDER Skip a little faster, or skip into a short run.
Band Glute Bridge↗
EASIER Lighter band, or no band at all.
HARDER Lift one foot off, or hold a moment at the top.
Crossover Step↗
EASIER Walk slowly through the crossover, one way then back.
HARDER Go faster and lower, or cover more ground each way.
Reactive 5m Sprint↗
EASIER Start on your own count, or run a shorter distance.
HARDER Run a little farther, or react to a surprise go-signal.
Banded Standing Adduction↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a smaller pull inward.
HARDER Heavier band, or move slower and more controlled.
Dead Bug with DB↗
EASIER Drop the weight and just use your arms and legs.
HARDER Hold a light dumbbell, or move both arms and legs together.
Jump Rope — Steady↗
EASIER Shorter turns, or step over the rope instead of jumping.
HARDER Jump a bit longer, or keep a quicker steady pace.
Bear Crawl Variations↗
EASIER Crawl forward only, slower, and a shorter distance.
HARDER Crawl farther, change directions, or carry a light weight.
Fast Feet / Ladder Equiv↗
EASIER Walk slowly through the foot pattern.
HARDER Quicker feet, or a slightly trickier pattern.
Reactive Lateral Start↗
EASIER Start on your own count, or use just one direction.
HARDER React to a surprise go-signal, or add more directions.
Deep Squat Hold↗
EASIER Hold onto something, or put a small lift under your heels.
HARDER Hold a little longer, or hug a light weight to your chest.
SL Balance — Eyes Closed↗
EASIER Open your eyes, or lightly touch something for balance.
HARDER Stand on a soft surface, or add a slow head turn.
Plank Shoulder Tap↗
EASIER Wider feet, or do it from the knees.
HARDER Bring the feet closer, or tap slower and more controlled.
Hollow Rock↗
EASIER Bend the knees and tuck in while you rock.
HARDER Reach arms all the way overhead, or hold a light weight.
Depth Drop (Low Box)↗
EASIER Lower box, or just step down and stick the landing.
HARDER Slightly higher box, or land on one foot.
A-Skip + March Combo↗
EASIER March only, or do it on the spot.
HARDER Finish by skipping into a short run.
Banded Front Squat↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a plain bodyweight squat.
HARDER Heavier band, or pause for a moment at the bottom.
Banded Chop / Lift↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a smaller motion.
HARDER Heavier band, do it standing, or add a slight twist.
Lateral Skater Bound↗
EASIER Hop side to side with a brief pause to stick each landing.
HARDER Cover more side-to-side distance, or add a surprise go-signal.
Single-Leg Pogo Hops↗
EASIER Hop on two feet, or keep the hops lower.
HARDER Bouncier or quicker hops, or hop while moving forward.
Crossover Step to Sprint↗
EASIER Walk slowly through the change into a jog.
HARDER Add a surprise go-signal, or sprint a little farther.
Reactive Mirror Sprint↗
EASIER Go slower, fewer directions, or have the leader signal early.
HARDER Bigger, faster moves, or add more directions to follow.
SL Box Step-Down↗
EASIER Lower box, or only step down part way.
HARDER Slightly higher box, or hold a light dumbbell.
Banded RDL↗
EASIER Lighter band, or hinge only part way down.
HARDER Heavier band, or pause at the bottom of the hinge.
Tall Plank DB Drag↗
EASIER Lighter weight, wider feet, or do it from the knees.
HARDER Heavier weight, or bring the feet closer together.
Jump Rope — Intervals↗
EASIER Shorter jumping, longer rests, or just steady skipping.
HARDER Longer or quicker jumping bouts, or shorter rests.
Multi-Direction Bound↗
EASIER Fewer directions, or stick each landing before the next.
HARDER Quicker changes, or react to a surprise go-signal.
A-Skip + B-Skip↗
EASIER Just the A-skip, or slowly march the B pattern.
HARDER Skip a little faster, or skip into a short run.
Reactive 4-Cone↗
EASIER Fewer cones, slower signals, or know the order ahead of time.
HARDER More cones, faster signals, or carry a puck.
Iso Wall Sit↗
EASIER Hold for less time, or sit a little higher.
HARDER Rest a light weight on your lap, or sit on one leg.
High-Low Pogo Combo↗
EASIER Steady hops that all stay the same height.
HARDER Hop on one foot, or switch heights more quickly.
Resisted → Free Sprint↗
EASIER Lighter resistance, or do the two parts separately.
HARDER Smooth the hand-off into the free run, or run farther free.
DB Goblet Lateral Lunge↗
EASIER No weight, or only lunge part way down.
HARDER Hold a heavier dumbbell, or pause at the bottom.
Sprint Intervals 15s↗
EASIER Fewer sprints, longer rests, or shorter sprints.
HARDER More sprints, shorter rests, or run each one a bit longer.
Jump Rope — Speed↗
EASIER Quick single spins, or shorter fast bursts.
HARDER Two spins per jump, or quicker bursts.
Crossover Step Bound↗
EASIER Slower crossover steps without a big push-off.
HARDER Quicker or longer bounds, or add a band for resistance.
Hill Stride↗
EASIER A gentler slope, or a shorter distance.
HARDER A steeper hill, or longer, quicker strides.
Plank to Push-Up↗
EASIER From the knees, or move slowly between positions.
HARDER Bring the feet closer, or slow the up-and-down on purpose.
20m Flying Sprint↗
EASIER Shorten to a 10 m fly, or run easy build-up strides.
HARDER Make the fly zone longer, or add a gentle downhill run.
Broad Jump↗
EASIER A shorter jump, or step back to reset each time.
HARDER Jump again right away, or jump into a short sprint.
20m Sprint↗
EASIER Short 10 m starts focused on the first hard pushes.
HARDER Add a band or sled at the start, or run out to 30 m.
Squat to Tuck Jump↗
EASIER A plain jump squat with no knee tuck, or lower jumps.
HARDER Tuck the knees higher, or jump again without resetting.
A-Skip Resisted↗
EASIER Lighter resistance, or skip with no resistance at all.
HARDER More resistance, or skip into a resisted short run.
Banded Squat Jump↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a plain bodyweight squat jump.
HARDER Heavier band, or hold a light weight as you jump.
Rotational Ball Toss↗
EASIER Lighter ball, or slower reps to groove the motion.
HARDER Heavier ball, or throw farther and more explosively.
Rotational Ball Side Toss↗
EASIER Lighter ball, or slower reps to groove the motion.
HARDER Heavier ball, or throw with more snap.
Mirror Sprint Game↗
EASIER Smaller space, slower leader, or fewer directions.
HARDER Bigger space, more directions, or add a puck or ball.
Banded Rotational Press↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a smaller motion.
HARDER Heavier band, or press with more snap.
SL CMJ Stick↗
EASIER A smaller jump, or jump and land on two feet.
HARDER Jump higher, or add a reach or light weight.
Squat to Broad Jump↗
EASIER A quicker pause, or a shorter jump.
HARDER A longer pause, a deeper squat, or hold a light weight.
Contrast Jump↗
EASIER A lighter warm-up move, or just the explosive jumps on their own.
HARDER Dial in the rest between moves, or use a heavier warm-up first.
Banded Rotational Throw (Wall)↗
EASIER Lighter band, or slower reps to groove the motion.
HARDER Heavier band, or throw with more snap.
Continuous Lateral Bound↗
EASIER Add a brief pause to stick each landing.
HARDER Cover more distance or reps, or clear a small target.
Band Rotational Scoop Toss↗
EASIER Lighter band, or a smaller motion.
HARDER Heavier band, or scoop with more snap.
Repeat Efforts — Shift Simulation↗
EASIER Fewer shifts, shorter bursts, or longer rests on the bench.
HARDER More shifts, longer bursts, or shorter rests between them.
Repeat Sprint Ability↗
EASIER Fewer sprints, longer rests, or more recovery between sets.
HARDER More sprints, or shorter rests between them.
09Recovery & Growth

WHERE KIDSget better.

Young athletes improve between sessions — when they sleep, eat, rest, and play. The training is the spark; recovery is where it actually sticks.

Sleep

9–11 hours a night for this age. Sleep is when the body grows and the brain locks in new skills. A good night’s sleep does more for a young athlete than any single drill.

Fuel

Regular meals with plenty of variety — protein, fruit and vegetables, and carbs for energy. Eat breakfast before a session, and have a snack and water afterward.

Play is training

Free play, other sports, and time at the park all build coordination and joy of movement. Encourage lots of it — variety makes better, healthier athletes than early specialization.

Rest days & listening in

Leave a day between sessions, and take extra rest when a child is tired, sore, or just not into it. A little muscle soreness is normal; sharp pain is a stop sign — and growing bodies need patience.

On a tough day

Some days the legs are heavy or the focus is off — that is normal. Cut the sets in half, pick the easier version of each move (see Make It Easier / Harder), keep the warm-up and the games, and finish on something fun. A short, happy session beats a long, grumpy one. If a child is unwell or in real pain, rest fully.

10How To Train This

SIXsimple rules.

For the player, the parent, and the coach. Follow these and the program takes care of itself.

01

Quality, not tiredness.

Every rep should look good and feel snappy. A few crisp reps with rest beat lots of sloppy, tired ones. If the movement gets messy, the set is done. The one planned exception is a short shift-style conditioning piece in the peak weeks (Weeks 6–7): there, staying fast even as you tire is the whole point — just like a real hockey shift. Everywhere else, quality comes first.

02

Have fun.

This is play with a purpose. Make games out of the drills, keep the energy up, and finish wanting more. A kid who enjoys it will keep coming back — that is the real win.

03

Stick every landing.

Soft, quiet, balanced landings are the most important skill at this age. “Land like a cat” protects young joints and builds the springs for later. Freeze and hold it.

04

Take the easy weeks.

Weeks 4 and 8 are lighter on purpose. Don’t add more because it feels easy — feeling fresh is exactly what lets the skills show up on the check.

05

Track your wins.

Fill in the skill-check pages. Seeing a jump get longer or a balance get steadier is huge motivation — proof the work is paying off.

06

Fit it around hockey.

Run the three sessions on non-skating or light-ice days. In a heavy game or tournament week, drop to two sessions — or just the warm-up and a game — and save the full block for lighter-ice stretches like the off-season.

11Your Coach

COACHDavid Ciboch.

David Ciboch is a strength and conditioning specialist with a Master’s in Sport Science and more than ten years coaching athletes from young beginners to the international level.

He serves as Strength & Conditioning coach for the UAE National Ice Hockey Team, and works day to day as a Physical Education and Sport Science teacher — so he coaches kids every week, not just pros. He founded Elite Hockey Drills to put genuine, expert-built off-ice training into the hands of every player and parent.

This program is the long-term-development work he would give a 9–11-year-old in his own gym: safe, fun, and built to grow a confident athlete — never a shrunk-down adult program.

CREDENTIALS
  • M.Ed. Sport Science
  • S&C Coach — UAE National Ice Hockey Team
  • 10+ years coaching, youth to international
  • PE & Sport Science teacher
  • Founder, Elite Hockey Drills
12What's Next

KEEPit going.

You built great habits in eight weeks. Here is how to keep them growing.

Run it back

At this age, repetition builds mastery. Run the eight weeks again — the same skills will look smoother and feel easier, and the jumps and sprints will keep climbing. Mix in plenty of free play and other sports.

The app

Every exercise demo is on the site today — just tap a name. The whole program, your skill-check pages, and more are coming to the Elite Hockey Drills app too, so it lives in your pocket and grows with the player.

Log your skill checks, keep landing soft, and have fun out there. Great athletes are built one good rep at a time.
